questions.question: When you run `temporal activity cancel` to request cancellation of an Activity that is currently heartbeating, how does the Activity actually learn it has been cancelled?

choices.text: Temporal immediately terminates the Activity process and marks it as cancelled right away.
A cancellation error is raised the next time the Activity sends a heartbeat and receives the response from Temporal.
The Activity is paused until a worker manually checks for pending cancellations and handles them.
Temporal waits for the current retry attempt to finish before delivering the cancellation signal.

questions.explanation: Requesting cancellation does not forcibly stop an Activity — it transitions its state to 'CancelRequested.' If the Activity is heartbeating, it only discovers this when it receives the response to its next heartbeat call, at which point a cancellation error is raised. This design lets long-running Activities stay in control of their own cleanup, rather than being killed mid-work. It's one reason heartbeating is so valuable: it keeps an Activity connected to Temporal so instructions like cancellation can reach it promptly.

questions.question: What is the difference between `temporal activity start` and `temporal activity execute` when working with Standalone Activities?

choices.text: `start` launches the Activity and returns immediately with the Activity ID, while `execute` launches the Activity and waits until it finishes before printing the result.
`start` requires a Workflow ID to attach to, while `execute` runs the Activity without any Workflow association.
`execute` skips retry logic entirely to speed things up, while `start` respects the configured retry policy.
`start` is only for Activities inside Workflows, while `execute` is the correct command for Standalone Activities.

questions.explanation: `temporal activity start` fires off a Standalone Activity and immediately outputs the Activity ID and Run ID — you don't have to wait for the work to finish. `temporal activity execute` does the same thing under the hood but then blocks until the Activity completes, printing the result to stdout. This mirrors a common pattern in software: fire-and-forget vs. synchronous wait. Use `execute` when you want a quick end-to-end test of an Activity from your terminal, and `start` when you want to kick something off and check on it separately.

questions.question: When you use `temporal activity reset` on an Activity that is currently executing, when does the reset actually take effect?

choices.text: The reset applies instantly, interrupting whatever the Activity is doing and restarting it from scratch immediately.
The reset is queued and only applies after all other pending Activities in the same Task Queue have finished.
The reset takes effect the next time the Activity fails, heartbeats, or times out — not while it is mid-execution.
The reset is ignored unless the Activity is already in a failed or timed-out state waiting for a retry.

questions.explanation: Temporal cannot safely reach into an actively running Activity and restart it in the middle — that would risk data corruption or inconsistent state. Instead, the reset is recorded and kicks in at the next natural stopping point: a failure, a heartbeat response, or a timeout. If the Activity is already waiting to retry (i.e., not currently running), the reset applies immediately. This is a great example of Temporal's philosophy: rather than forcibly killing work, it coordinates cleanly through well-defined lifecycle events.

questions.question: You run `temporal activity cancel --activity-id myActivity` against a running Activity that is heartbeating. What happens next?

choices.text: The Activity is immediately terminated and moved to a canceled status without waiting for the next heartbeat.

The Activity's run state transitions to CancelRequested, and a cancellation error is raised when the next heartbeat response is received — if the Activity lets that error propagate, it moves to canceled status.
The Activity is paused until it receives the cancel signal, at which point the Worker decides whether to honor it based on its retry policy.
The cancel command schedules the Activity for termination at the end of its current start-to-close timeout, regardless of heartbeating.

questions.explanation: Requesting cancellation transitions the Activity's run state to CancelRequested — it does NOT immediately stop the Activity. For heartbeating Activities, the cancellation error is surfaced on the next heartbeat call. Only if the Activity allows that error to propagate does it actually move to canceled status. This is an important distinction: cancel is cooperative, not forceful. Option A describes termination, not cancellation. Option C confuses cancel with pause semantics. Option D misattributes the behavior to the start-to-close timeout.

questions.question: A developer wants to mark a workflow-invoked Activity as failed from the CLI for testing purposes. Which command and flags are correct?

choices.text: `temporal activity fail --activity-id myAct --workflow-id myWf` — both flags are required because `--workflow-id` is mandatory for workflow Activities.
`temporal activity fail --activity-id myAct` — `--workflow-id` is optional and the CLI will locate the Activity across all workflows automatically.
`temporal activity complete --activity-id myAct --workflow-id myWf --result '{"error":true}'` — the `complete` command accepts error results via JSON, so a separate `fail` command is not needed.
`temporal activity fail --activity-id myAct --run-id myRunId` — `--run-id` substitutes for `--workflow-id` when targeting workflow Activities.

questions.explanation: For workflow Activities, `--workflow-id` is required when using `temporal activity fail`. The docs state: 'Workflow ID. Required for workflow Activities. Omit for Standalone Activities.' Option B is wrong — the CLI cannot search across all workflows without an explicit workflow ID. Option C misuses `complete`; passing error data to `complete` marks the Activity as successfully finished, not failed. Option D is wrong — `--run-id` in this context is the Workflow Run ID, an optional refinement, not a replacement for `--workflow-id`.

questions.question: You run `temporal activity reset --activity-id myAct --workflow-id myWf --reset-heartbeats` on an Activity that is currently executing. When does the reset take effect?

choices.text: Immediately — the currently running attempt is forcefully interrupted and a fresh attempt begins with cleared heartbeat details.
The next time the Activity fails, heartbeats, or times out — at that point the attempt count and heartbeat details are cleared and the Activity is rescheduled from scratch.
After the current start-to-close timeout expires — the server waits for the timeout before applying the reset.
Only after the Activity is manually paused first — reset cannot be applied to an actively executing Activity without pausing it.

questions.explanation: The docs clearly state: 'If the activity may be executing (i.e. it has not yet timed out), the reset will take effect the next time it fails, heartbeats, or times out.' The reset is not immediate for running activities; it is deferred to the next natural state transition. Additionally, `--reset-heartbeats` (which requires `--reset-attempts`) will clear heartbeat details at that point. Option A describes forceful termination, which is not how reset works. Option C ties the timing to the timeout specifically, which is only one of the three triggers. Option D fabricates a pausing prerequisite that does not exist.

questions.question: An on-call engineer receives an alert: a critical data-pipeline Activity has been running for 6 hours, is stuck heartbeating, and needs to be stopped immediately so a corrected version can rerun from scratch. The Activity is invoked by a Workflow (not a Standalone Activity). The engineer runs `temporal activity cancel --activity-id pipeline-act-001`. What actually happens next, and what must the Activity code do for the cancellation to fully take effect?

choices.text: The server immediately kills the Activity worker goroutine and marks the Activity as Canceled without any action required from the Activity code itself.

The cancel command transitions the Activity's state to CancelRequested; the Activity code must observe the cancellation error raised on the next heartbeat call and allow it to propagate for the Activity to reach Canceled status.
The cancel command only works for Standalone Activities; for Workflow-invoked Activities the engineer must cancel the entire Workflow run to stop the Activity.
The cancel command marks the Activity as Canceled immediately, but the Workflow will not be notified until the Activity's start-to-close timeout expires and it schedules a new attempt.

questions.explanation: Cancellation in Temporal is cooperative, not preemptive. `temporal activity cancel` transitions the Activity's run state to CancelRequested on the server side. The running Activity code only learns about this at its next heartbeat call, which raises a CancellationError (or equivalent in the SDK language). If the Activity catches that error and does NOT re-throw it, the Activity will keep running and the cancellation has no effect. For the Activity to actually reach Canceled status, the code must let the cancellation error propagate (or explicitly re-throw it after any needed cleanup). The CLI command is not restricted to Standalone Activities for cancel — the `--activity-id` flag targets the Activity within its Workflow context when `--workflow-id` is also provided.

questions.question: A payment-processing Activity is currently paused (via `temporal activity pause`) after repeated failures. The team has deployed a fix and wants to restart the Activity from the beginning — resetting attempt count and clearing any accumulated heartbeat details — without leaving it in a paused state. Which single CLI command and flag combination achieves this correctly?

choices.text: `temporal activity unpause --activity-id pay-act --workflow-id pay-wf` followed by a separate `temporal activity reset --reset-attempts --activity-id pay-act --workflow-id pay-wf` to clear heartbeats.

`temporal activity reset --activity-id pay-act --workflow-id pay-wf --reset-attempts --reset-heartbeats`, which unpauses by default and clears both attempt count and heartbeat details in one operation.

`temporal activity reset --activity-id pay-act --workflow-id pay-wf --keep-paused --reset-heartbeats`, which clears heartbeat details and unpauses the Activity automatically when --reset-heartbeats is set.
`temporal activity complete --activity-id pay-act --workflow-id pay-wf --result '{}'` to clear the paused state, then restart the Activity by re-signaling the Workflow.

questions.explanation: `temporal activity reset` restarts an Activity as if it were first scheduled, resetting attempt count and timeouts. By default, if the Activity is paused, reset also unpauses it — so no separate unpause is needed. The `--reset-heartbeats` flag additionally clears accumulated heartbeat details, but it only works when `--reset-attempts` is also specified. Importantly, using `--keep-paused` would prevent unpausing, which is the opposite of what the team wants. Option A requires two commands unnecessarily. Option C misreads the flag semantics: `--keep-paused` preserves the paused state rather than removing it. Option D abuses `complete` and would record a spurious success event, corrupting the Workflow's business logic.

questions.question: A team is running bulk Activity resets across thousands of Workflow executions using `temporal activity reset --query 'TemporalResetInfo="property:activityType=IngestData"'`. Their Temporal cluster is under high load and they are concerned about overwhelming the frontend service. Which flag should they use to throttle the operation, and what is the constraint on when that flag is valid?

choices.text: Use `--jitter` to spread resets randomly over a time window; this flag is valid both with `--workflow-id` and `--query`.
Use `--page-size` to limit how many Workflow executions are fetched per server request; this flag is available on all `activity reset` invocations regardless of other flags.
Use `--rps` to cap the batch's requests per second; this flag is only allowed when `--query` is present, which matches this bulk-reset scenario.
Use `--priority-key` set to 5 (lowest priority) to reduce scheduling pressure; this flag downgrades the batch operation's server-side priority automatically.

questions.explanation: The `--rps` flag on `temporal activity reset` limits the rate of requests the batch operation sends to the server, directly protecting the cluster under load. Crucially, the documentation states it is only allowed when `--query` is present — which aligns with this bulk-reset scenario where `--query` is already being used. `--jitter` spreads individual activity reset timing randomly within a duration window, but the docs note it can only be used with `--query` and affects when each reset fires, not the request rate to the server. `--page-size` does not exist on `activity reset`. `--priority-key` is a flag on `activity start`/`execute` for task dispatch priority, not a batch throttle mechanism.

questions.question: An engineer wants to manually complete a Workflow-invoked Activity (id: `checkout-act`) that is stuck in a retry loop, injecting a synthetic success result so the Workflow can proceed. They also want to complete a Standalone Activity (id: `audit-act`) with a result. How do the required flags differ between these two `temporal activity complete` invocations?

choices.text: Both invocations require `--workflow-id`. For the Standalone Activity, `--workflow-id` refers to the parent Workflow that launched it; for the Workflow-invoked Activity it refers to the owning Workflow run.

The Workflow-invoked Activity requires `--workflow-id` (the owning Workflow's ID) plus `--activity-id` and `--result`; the Standalone Activity omits `--workflow-id` and uses `--run-id` as the Activity Run ID instead of Workflow Run ID.

Both invocations require only `--activity-id` and `--result`; `--workflow-id` is optional for Workflow-invoked Activities and is used only to disambiguate when multiple Workflows share an Activity ID.
The Standalone Activity requires `--workflow-id` set to a special sentinel value of `standalone`; the Workflow-invoked Activity uses the actual Workflow ID.

questions.explanation: The `temporal activity complete` command behaves differently depending on whether the Activity is Workflow-invoked or Standalone. For a Workflow-invoked Activity, `--workflow-id` is required to identify the owning Workflow, and `--run-id` (if provided) is interpreted as the Workflow Run ID. For a Standalone Activity, `--workflow-id` must be omitted entirely, and `--run-id` (if provided) is interpreted as the Activity Run ID rather than a Workflow Run ID. This distinction matters operationally: supplying `--workflow-id` for a Standalone Activity or omitting it for a Workflow-invoked Activity will cause the command to fail or target the wrong execution. Both cases require `--activity-id` and `--result` as mandatory flags.

questions.question:
A platform engineer is operating a long-running Standalone Activity that heartbeats every 30 seconds to checkpoint progress. While the activity is mid-execution, they need to reset it due to corrupted heartbeat state. They run:

```
temporal activity reset \
    --activity-id ProcessDataActivity \
    --workflow-id wf-001 \
    --reset-heartbeats
```

However, their activity code does not handle the `CancelledFailure` that is delivered on the next heartbeat call after a reset. What actually happens, and what is the interaction between the reset mechanism and heartbeat delivery?

choices.text: The reset takes effect immediately regardless of execution state, forcibly terminating the running attempt and scheduling a fresh attempt. Because `--reset-heartbeats` was specified without `--reset-attempts`, the attempt counter is preserved but heartbeat details are cleared, so the next attempt starts with no checkpoint data.

The reset is deferred until the currently-running attempt next heartbeats, fails, or times out. At the next heartbeat call, the SDK delivers a `CancelledFailure` to the activity code. Since the activity does not handle this error, it propagates as an unhandled failure, causing that attempt to fail. The reset then applies — clearing heartbeat details and resetting the attempt count — and a new attempt is scheduled per the retry policy.

The `--reset-heartbeats` flag is only valid when the activity is already in a waiting-for-retry state. Because the activity is actively running, the CLI command returns an error and no reset is applied. The operator must wait for the current attempt to complete or time out before issuing the reset command.

The reset is applied immediately to the server-side activity record, clearing heartbeat details and attempt count. The currently-executing attempt continues running and will eventually complete or fail independently. If it completes successfully before the reset takes effect on the worker, the completion is accepted and the reset is discarded.

questions.explanation:
This question exercises the interaction between activity reset, heartbeat delivery, and failure propagation. When a running activity is reset, Temporal does not forcibly kill the in-flight attempt — it cannot, because the worker is executing outside of Temporal's direct control. Instead, the reset is recorded server-side and takes effect the next time the activity 'checks in': on the next heartbeat response, failure, or timeout.

At the next heartbeat call after a reset, the server returns a `CancelledFailure` to the SDK, which surfaces it as an error in the activity's heartbeat call. This is the designed mechanism for notifying long-running activities that they should stop and clean up. The documentation explicitly states: 'Activities that heartbeat will receive a Canceled failure the next time they heartbeat after a reset. If, in your Activity, you need to do any cleanup when an Activity is reset, handle this error and then re-throw it when you've cleaned up.'

If the activity does not catch and handle this `CancelledFailure`, it propagates as an unhandled exception/error, and that attempt is recorded as failed. The reset then applies — resetting attempts and clearing heartbeat details if `--reset-heartbeats` was specified — and the retry policy determines whether a new attempt is scheduled.

The production insight here is critical: `--reset-heartbeats` requires `--reset-attempts` to work (per the CLI docs: 'Only works with --reset-attempts'), so omitting `--reset-attempts` means heartbeat details are NOT cleared despite the flag. Engineers must pair these flags consciously. More broadly, reset is not a hard kill; it is a cooperative interrupt delivered through the heartbeat channel. Activity code that swallows all exceptions or never heartbeats will not respond to resets promptly, which can leave stale activity attempts running well past the reset command.
